bibliographic_data.title: Issues in the Formation of Globular Cluster Systems
bibliographic_data.abstract: Globular clusters make up less than 1% of the visible halo stars, but contain information about the history of halo formation out of all proportion to their fraction of the total mass. The literature on globular cluster systems in the Milky Way and other galaxies is now far too large to summarize in one short review, so I will concentrate only on a few recent issues that have taken on particular importance. Within our own Milky Way, globular cluster ages have once again taken center stage in the debate over halo formation. With continuing improvements in stellar physics and evolution codes, the best estimates of absolute ages for the oldest globular clusters have undergone modest downward revisions. Independently of absolute age calibrations, we can estimate the duration of the halo formation era by finding relative cluster ages from widely different parts of the halo and over the full range of cluster metallicities.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper discusses recent issues in the formation of globular clusters, particularly focusing on their ages and kinematical distributions within the Milky Way.
core_content.key_methodology: The author reviews recent studies and data to estimate relative cluster ages and discuss the implications for the formation history of the Milky Way's halo.
outcomes: Relative cluster ages can be gauged to within ±0.5 Gyr for clusters of similar compositions.
The most metal-poor globular clusters have identical ages, suggesting that globular cluster and halo-star formation began at virtually the same time across the protogalaxy.
core_content.full_text_markdown: Globular clusters make up less than 1% of the visible halo stars but contain information about the history of halo formation out of all proportion to their fraction of the total mass. The literature on globular cluster systems in the Milky Way and other galaxies is now far too large to summarize in one short review, so this paper will concentrate only on a few recent issues that have taken on particular importance. Within our own Milky Way, globular cluster ages have once again taken center stage in the debate over halo formation. With continuing improvements in stellar physics and evolution codes, the best estimates of absolute ages for the oldest globular clusters have undergone modest downward revisions. Independently of absolute age calibrations, we can estimate the duration of the halo formation era by finding relative cluster ages from widely different parts of the halo and over the full range of cluster metallicities. Various recent discussions suggest that there is an overall age-metallicity relation, with more metal-rich clusters being ∼2 Gyr younger on average than the most metal-poor (and presumably oldest) ones like M92 and M15. But perhaps surprisingly, we are discovering that the ages of the lowest-metallicity clusters are identical to within measurement precision everywhere in the halo from Galactocentric distances of ∼5 kpc out to ∼100 kpc.
